concept_name: Detecting a Change of Direction on Motion Graphs
source_book: DC Pandey Chapter 7.5 — identifying direction reversal on x-t and v-t graphs

real_world_anchor.primary: A Delhi Metro train ping-ponging between Rajiv Chowk and New Delhi stations during a signal fault. The driver moves the train forward, stops, reverses to clear the platform, and then goes forward again. On the x-t graph this is a line that rises, flattens, DROPS, flattens, and rises again. Every peak is the moment the train reverses. That reversal is unmistakable on the graph because the slope changes sign at those peaks.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A Mumbai lift in a ten-storey office: it goes up to floor 8, pauses, then comes back down to floor 2 to pick someone up, then goes up to floor 10. The x-t graph is the building's floor number plotted against time. Peaks on the graph mark the two turning moments — when the lift reverses. Reading these reversals from the slope change is a core exam skill.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A tennis ball thrown straight up from a cricket ground at Eden Gardens. Going up, velocity is positive. At the peak of the throw, velocity is momentarily zero. On the way down, velocity becomes negative. The v-t graph is a single straight line that crosses the time axis at the peak. The crossing moment is the reversal.

formulas.x_t_reversal: On x-t graph, direction reverses at each local maximum or minimum — slope changes sign at that point.
formulas.v_t_reversal: On v-t graph, direction reverses wherever the curve crosses the time axis — v passes through zero and flips sign.
formulas.v_zero_at_turnaround: At the moment of reversal, instantaneous velocity is zero, not minimum speed.

constraints: A peak on the x-t graph = object reaches its furthest position in that direction, then starts moving back
A trough on the x-t graph = object reaches its furthest position backward, then starts moving forward
v-t crossing t-axis with slope non-zero = clean sign flip of velocity
v-t touching t-axis and bouncing back (not crossing) = momentary stop without reversal

STATE_1.title: Peak on x-t Graph = Direction Reverses

scene_composition.text:
x-t graph with a direction reversal

x |           _
  |         /   \
  |       /       \                   
  |     /           \____
  |    /                  \
  |   /                     \
  |__/_________________________\___   t
  0    up phase      peak     back

  Moving up:  slope positive -> moving +x
  At the peak:  slope is zero momentarily
  After peak:  slope negative -> moving -x

  That sign flip of the SLOPE is the direction reversal.
Delhi Metro: forward -> stop -> reverse to clear platform

tts_sentences.text_en: The clearest sign that an object has reversed direction is a peak — or a trough — on its x-t graph.
Before the peak, the slope is positive — the object is moving forward.
Exactly at the peak, the slope is zero — the object is momentarily stationary.
After the peak, the slope is negative — the object is moving backward.
Picture a Delhi Metro driver moving forward to the signal, stopping, and reversing to clear the platform. That trip draws a perfect peak on the x-t graph.

STATE_2.title: On the v-t Graph: Crossing the Time Axis

scene_composition.text:
v-t graph with a direction reversal

v |
  |   \
  |     \          
  |       \     
  |_________\__________________  t
  |          \  
  |           \
  |             \
  |              \

  Crossing the t-axis means:
    velocity was +  (moving +x)
    velocity passes through 0
    velocity becomes -  (moving -x)

  That IS the reversal, plain as day.

  On a cricket six the ball's v-t line is straight with slope -g.
  It crosses the t-axis at the peak of the throw.
Tennis ball thrown up on a cricket ground

tts_sentences.text_en: On the velocity-time graph, a direction reversal is even more direct to spot.
Wherever the v-t line actually crosses the time axis — passing through zero with non-zero slope — that is the reversal moment.
A cricketer tosses a tennis ball vertically upwards. Its v-t graph is a perfectly straight line sloping downward with slope minus g.
The moment that line cuts the t-axis is the peak of the throw — velocity momentarily zero, and then negative on the descent.

STATE_3.title: Touching vs. Crossing — A Subtle Distinction

scene_composition.text:
Touching the axis vs. crossing the axis

v |  \                          v |  \               /
  |    \                          |    \           /
  |      \       no reversal      |      \       /
  |        \_    (v stops and     |        \   /
  |________.........._____  t     |_________ V ____________ t
  |           bounces back)       |           crossing
  |
  LEFT   v-t touches axis then     RIGHT   v-t cuts through axis
         climbs back (same sign)           -> sign flip -> reversal

  Real-world example:
    LEFT:   a train braking to a halt at a station,
            then starting again in the SAME direction.
    RIGHT:  a ball tossed up, then falling back — direction reverses.
Key: look at the SIGN OF v just before and just after t=t_stop

tts_sentences.text_en: Now a subtlety worth one exam mark — touching versus crossing the time axis.
A train braking at a station reaches zero velocity and then starts again in the same forward direction. Its v-t graph dips to zero and climbs back — but it does NOT cross the axis.
That is a momentary stop without reversal.
A ball tossed up crosses the axis cleanly — above the axis, then below — because the ball literally reverses direction.
The test is simple: compare the sign of velocity just before and just after the zero. Same sign — no reversal. Opposite signs — reversal.

epic_c_branches.misconception_summary: Student thinks that any time velocity reads zero on the graph, the object has stopped for good.
confusion_patterns: at peak the object is permanently at rest

scene_composition.text:
Student's wrong reading:

v |  \
  |    \
  |      \                       ~ instant of v = 0
  |________\__________________  t
  |         
  |         

  "Velocity has become zero,
   so the ball must be at rest forever."

  But zero velocity is only an INSTANT.
  It tells us nothing about what happens next.

tts_sentences.text_en: A common misreading: the moment v hits zero, the student concludes the object has stopped forever.
This collapses three very different physical situations into one.
Zero velocity is just an instant — a point on the graph. It says absolutely nothing about the next second.

STATE_2.title: Three Different Futures from the Same v = 0

scene_composition.text:
Three futures after v = 0:

 1. Ball at peak:  v just after = negative
                   -> ball falls back  (REVERSAL)
                   Example: cricket toss.

 2. Train at stop: v just after = 0 again
                   -> truly at rest  (PERMANENT STOP)
                   Example: bus parked, engine off.

 3. Train at station: v just after = positive
                      -> resumes forward motion (TEMPORARY STOP)
                      Example: Metro paused at platform.

  The ONLY way to tell them apart is to look at the
  slope (acceleration) at the moment v = 0.

tts_sentences.text_en: The same instant of v = 0 can mean three completely different things.
First, a ball at the peak of its toss — v becomes zero, then gravity pulls it back, so it reverses.
Second, a truly parked vehicle — v stays zero forever because there is no net force to restart motion.
Third, a Metro train paused at a platform — v is briefly zero, but the driver presses the accelerator again and forward motion resumes, no reversal.
Only a look at what happens IMMEDIATELY after t-equals-stop can distinguish these.

STATE_3.title: Rule of Thumb: Check the Slope Where v = 0

scene_composition.text:
At the moment v = 0:

   slope (a) non-zero   ->  direction WILL reverse in the next instant
                            (e.g. ball at peak)

   slope (a) exactly 0   -> object remains at rest
                            (e.g. parked car, no net force)

   slope (a) positive   ->  velocity becomes positive next instant
   slope (a) negative   ->  velocity becomes negative next instant

  Memorise: velocity tells you WHERE the object is going right now.
           acceleration tells you WHERE it is going next.
v tells you NOW, a tells you NEXT

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the shortcut. At any moment v equals zero, look at the ACCELERATION.
If the acceleration is non-zero, the object WILL move in the direction of acceleration in the next instant — so it reverses if it was moving oppositely.
If the acceleration is also zero, the object is truly at rest and stays at rest.
Burn this into memory: velocity tells you where the object is going right now. Acceleration tells you where it is going next.
